author: @georgemathieson6097
text:
All you need now to perfect your videos are: lapel mic - as someone else has already suggested -, a second and/ or third camera angle (one facing downward onto your work surface, one slightly more to your left), a fade out in the intro music, maybe occasional subtitles (sub-headings) for import points, etc. In other words just a bit more heavy editing (by the way, I really appreciate the 1080p footage you upload these days). I'm being *extremely* picky there simply because you're videos are absolutely amazing. You're always inspiring me and further educating me, and I can't thank you enough. You're videos speak for themselves. You might as well ignore my advice because I'm suggesting improvements to someone with millions of views more than myself. Keep up the hard work, and I can't wait to see plenty more projects for years to come. 


(I'm a student currently studying A-Level Physics, Maths, Computer Science, and Electronics. I'd love to get in contact and discuss a few things that will help me with my own projects. My email address is 14gmathieson@gmail.com)

author: @SI-GOD
text:
Excellent video. What are the practical applications for this motor? I realize it can be made to a larger size but does it have enough torque to overcome Lens Law of you attached magnets and generator coils? Or can it be made strong enough to turn any external things such as a water pump or alternator/generator?
Also, please edit videos to speed up repetitive task like drawing lines after the 2ed line is shown or cutting out the disk.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Not likely it is just science fun for recruiting new blood into the STEMs.

replies.author: @SI-GOD
replies.text: @Barskor1 I see. I think it is fascination but I'm wanting to discover technologies for power production that the average person can do without fossil fuel on a very low budget. This far, I like best the Bidini motor and the Searl Effect Generator. The SEG is unattainable for most due to the cost of precision milling that is needed and as of yet had not been directly duplicated (to my knowledge). A bidini motor can be done easily by anyone with basic mechanical skills using scraps available in most people's home.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It really is a science fair project mate - this isn't a source of serious power - there are some interesting concepts to look at here but for more power you are going to need different materials that exhibit the same responses but more powerfully like nitinol for example

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Barskor1 spot on mate

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text:
@SI-GOD Well IMO seeking an over-unity generator is an improbable goal to get done anything running an electric motor to get more electricity is not likely to work.
The better option is to look at the pure generator side of things just looking at the mechanics and electromagnetic fields an area I think is under studied is the windings and how they are tight coils if you were pushing a fluid trying to get maximum flow and lest resistance they would be nearly the worst thing you could do. Treating electrons like water may be wrong but constantly changing the direction of motion must cost some energy even for electrons.
With that in mind picture 100 wires in parallel the ends attached to bus bars half moon curving over the rotating magnet and another one for the other side you can layer the wires as deep as needed to obtain the best capture of the field.
 This probably has been thought of or done by others but it is on my bucket list to get done as a project.
